frameworks.name: Sales Director Role Definition
frameworks.definition: A sales director's primary function is culture maintenance and daily sales training — not closing deals or carrying specialized domain knowledge about the product being sold.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that an overly consultative sales process requires unicorn-caliber salespeople to execute; simplifying the script to a straight-line process opens the talent pool to any capable seller and removes the dependency on rare specialized hires.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: A consultative process demands contextual judgment at every step; a scripted straight-line process converts judgment into procedure, meaning any rep who can follow a script and redirect off-topic questions back to the line can execute it — no specialized domain knowledge required.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the product requires genuine technical discovery to scope (e.g., custom software builds)
Hormozi acknowledges the agency's current numbers are healthy — fix is for TAM expansion, not conversion repair

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that for a done-for-you client-acquisition agency at $5M revenue (31 clients, ~$150k/year average contract), a sales director costs approximately $60,000 to acquire (CAC) and generates approximately $600,000 in lifetime gross profit — a roughly 10:1 ROI ratio that justifies aggressive investment in recruiting spend.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: worth to you in lifetime gross profit

numbers.axis: Sales director investment vs. lifetime gross profit return

bounded_by: Assumes the sales director successfully manages ~3 client accounts
Gross profit figure, not revenue — margin assumptions not stated

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the true bottleneck for this agency's sales director shortage is not talent scarcity in the market but the belief that a director needs rare specialized skills — the agency's consultative sales process artificially inflates the skill requirement, shrinking the candidate pool.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Agency with consultative, non-scripted sales process
Role requires managing a sales team, not closing deals personally
claims.mechanism: When a sales process is consultative and relies on situational judgment, hiring managers conclude they need directors with deep domain expertise; fixing the script converts the role requirement to general sales management skill, which is abundant in the market.
bounded_by: Applies specifically when the director role is training and culture, not personal closing

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that sales directors in a scaled agency should function as culture carriers and daily training drivers, not as closers or product experts — they likely need less domain knowledge than frontline sales reps.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Agency sales team where directors manage multiple teams (10-15 reps per director)
Directors are not required to take sales calls
claims.mechanism: Teaching sales is a generalizable skill; if a director never closes deals personally, domain knowledge of the product being sold is irrelevant to their job performance.
bounded_by: Does not apply to player-coach sales directors who also carry a quota

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a single sales director managing 15 salespeople is above the comfortable span-of-control ceiling; his preference is approximately 10 reps per director, with splitting to two directors at 7-8 reps each as a preferable interim structure.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Sales teams where directors are responsible for training and culture
Remote or distributed sales environment implied

numbers.axis: Sales reps per director (span of control)

bounded_by: Hormozi explicitly notes this is his personal preference, not a hard rule

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that door-to-door sales veterans are an undertapped talent pool for agency sales director roles, because the skill of building and managing a field sales team transfers directly — the primary training gap is language and price point, not sales fundamentals.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Agency sales director role where director manages and trains reps, does not close personally
Product is sold remotely (phone/video) not in-field
claims.mechanism: Door-to-door veterans have already built the core muscle of recruiting, training, and managing high-volume sales teams under pressure; the incremental learning required to adapt to an agency context (vocabulary, price point) is materially smaller than learning sales management from scratch.
bounded_by: Requires that the existing sales script is straightforward enough to teach quickly
Price point adaptation is acknowledged as a real (if minor) gap

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that this agency's underlying sales economics are already sound ($60k CAC in, $600k GP out), so the correct lever is not to optimize spend but to fix the process upstream to expand the addressable talent pool — the constraint is psychological (belief about who qualifies) not economic.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Business where unit economics on key hires are already strongly positive
Talent pool appears constrained but process has not been audited
claims.mechanism: When ROI per hire is clearly positive, the correct action is to increase volume and widen the pool, not to protect spend; the only reason not to scale spend is if the process itself is the real bottleneck masquerading as a talent shortage.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the Acquisition.com/Gym Launch sales handbook training process is directly transferable as a sales director onboarding curriculum for this agency's team structure, requiring no custom development.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: the sales handbook that we have at the launch
conditions: Sales directors whose primary responsibility is training and culture
Team structure similar to Gym Launch (daily training-driven model)
bounded_by: Conditional on the agency first fixing its script to be less consultative

examples.text: The agency in this session runs done-for-you client acquisition for marketing agencies (Amazon, email, paid media). At $5M revenue across 31 clients averaging ~$150k/year, each sales director manages roughly 3 agency client accounts and oversees a team of 5-6 reps (15 reps total per director). The operator spends $15,000/month recruiting sales directors, achieving a $60,000 CAC per director hired. Despite a 10:1 gross-profit ROI on each hire, the operator reports the qualified talent pool is shrinking over time, which Hormozi diagnoses as a process problem — the consultative sales script requires rare specialized salespeople — rather than a genuine market scarcity of sales managers.

Hormozi cites his own sales team as a contrasting example: the average age of Acquisition.com's salespeople is approximately 23, and they carry no deep knowledge of the client businesses they sell for. They succeed purely by following a scripted process and redirecting off-script questions back to the straight-line path. This illustrates that specialized domain knowledge is not a prerequisite for high sales performance when the script is tight.

practices.text: Audit your sales script before attributing a talent shortage to thin market supply — a consultative or judgment-heavy script artificially inflates the skill requirement for every role in the org.

When recruiting for sales director roles focused on training and culture (not closing), source candidates from door-to-door sales management backgrounds where team-building and high-volume rep management are already proven.
Target a span of control of approximately 10 reps per sales director; split into two directors at 7-8 reps each rather than letting one director manage 15.
Use a proven sales training handbook (e.g., Gym Launch sales handbook) as the director onboarding curriculum rather than building a custom program from scratch.
